The [national flag](/wiki/National_flag "National flag") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"),[[a]](#cite_note-2) popularly referred to as the **Maple Leaf**,[[b]](#cite_note-3) consists of a red field with a white square at its centre in the horizontal ratio of 1∶2∶1, in which is featured one stylized, red, 11-pointed [maple leaf](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") [charged](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)") in the centre.[[2]](#cite_note-4) It is the first [flag](/wiki/Flag "Flag") to have been adopted by both houses of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") and officially [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") by the [Canadian monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") as the country's official [national flag](/wiki/National_flag "National flag").[[3]](#cite_note-canada-history-5) The flag has become the predominant and most recognizable [national symbol of Canada](/wiki/National_symbol_of_Canada "National symbol of Canada").

In 1964, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") formed a committee to resolve the ongoing issue of the lack of an official Canadian flag, sparking a [debate about a flag change](/wiki/Great_Canadian_flag_debate "Great Canadian flag debate") to replace the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag"). Out of three choices, the maple leaf design by [Mount Allison University](/wiki/Mount_Allison_University "Mount Allison University") historian [George Stanley](/wiki/George_Stanley "George Stanley"),[[4]](#cite_note-6) based on the flag of the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada"), was selected. The flag officially appeared on February 15, 1965; the date is now celebrated annually as [National Flag of Canada Day](/wiki/National_Flag_of_Canada_Day "National Flag of Canada Day").

Before 1965, the [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign") had been in unofficial use since the 1860s and was later officially approved by a 1945 [Order in Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council") for use "wherever place or occasion may make it desirable to fly a distinctive Canadian flag".[[5]](#cite_note-1945_order-7)[[6]](#cite_note-first_flags-8) Also, the [Royal Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") remains an official flag in Canada, to symbolize Canada's allegiance to the monarch and membership in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[7]](#cite_note-9) There is no law dictating how the national flag is to be treated, but there are conventions and protocols to guide how it is to be displayed and its place in the order of precedence of flags, which gives it primacy over the aforementioned and most other flags.

Many different flags created by Canadian officials, government bodies, and military forces contain the maple leaf motif in some fashion, either by having the Canadian flag charged in the canton or by including maple leaves in the design. The Canadian flag also appears on the government's [wordmark](/wiki/Federal_Identity_Program "Federal Identity Program").

## Origins and design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins and design")]

See also: [Maple leaf](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Flag_of_Canada_%28construction_sheet_-_leaf_geometry%29.svg/330px-Flag_of_Canada_%28construction_sheet_-_leaf_geometry%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Canada_(construction_sheet_-_leaf_geometry).svg)

Construction sheet for the national flag of Canada

The flag is horizontally symmetric, so the obverse and reverse sides appear identical. The width of the Maple Leaf flag is twice the height. The white field is a [Canadian pale](/wiki/Canadian_pale "Canadian pale") (a central band occupying half the width of a vertical [triband flag](/wiki/Triband_(flag) "Triband (flag)"), rather than a third of the width, named for its use in this flag);[[8]](#cite_note-10) each bordering red field is exactly half its size[[9]](#cite_note-colours-11) and it bears a stylized red maple leaf at its centre. In heraldic terminology, the flag's [blazon](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon") as outlined on the original royal proclamation is "*[gules](/wiki/Gules "Gules") on a [Canadian pale](/wiki/Canadian_pale "Canadian pale") [argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent") a maple leaf of the first*".[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-14)

The [maple leaf](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") has been a Canadian emblem since the 19th century.[[13]](#cite_note-Minahan2009-15) It was first used as a national symbol in 1868 when it appeared on the [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of both [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec").[[14]](#cite_note-HannaMiddleton2008-16) In 1867, [Alexander Muir](/wiki/Alexander_Muir "Alexander Muir") composed the patriotic song "[The Maple Leaf Forever](/wiki/The_Maple_Leaf_Forever "The Maple Leaf Forever")", which became an unofficial [anthem](/wiki/Anthem "Anthem") in English-speaking Canada.[[15]](#cite_note-Irr1998-17) The maple leaf was later added to the [Canadian coat of arms](/wiki/Canadian_coat_of_arms "Canadian coat of arms") in 1921.[[14]](#cite_note-HannaMiddleton2008-16) From 1876 until 1901, the leaf appeared on all [Canadian coins](/wiki/Canadian_coins "Canadian coins") and remained on the [penny](/wiki/Penny_(Canadian_coin) "Penny (Canadian coin)") after 1901.[[16]](#cite_note-Cross2011-18) The use of the maple leaf by the [100th (Prince of Wales's Royal Canadian) Regiment of Foot](/wiki/100th_(Prince_of_Wales%27s_Royal_Canadian)_Regiment_of_Foot "100th (Prince of Wales's Royal Canadian) Regiment of Foot") as a [regimental symbol](/wiki/Regimental_symbol "Regimental symbol") extended back to 1860.[[17]](#cite_note-Herd2012-19) During the [First](/wiki/Military_history_of_Canada_during_World_War_I "Military history of Canada during World War I") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/Canada_in_World_War_II "Canada in World War II"), badges of the Canadian forces were often based on a maple leaf design.[[18]](#cite_note-Granatstein201-20) The maple leaf would eventually adorn the tombstones of Canadian military graves.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Arms_of_Ontario.svg/250px-Arms_of_Ontario.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arms_of_Ontario.svg)

[Arms of Ontario](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Ontario "Coat of arms of Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Coat_of_arms_of_Qu%C3%A9bec_%281867-1939%29.svg/250px-Coat_of_arms_of_Qu%C3%A9bec_%281867-1939%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_Qu%C3%A9bec_(1867-1939).svg)

[Arms of Quebec](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Quebec "Coat of arms of Quebec")

Maple leaves have been used on the arms of Ontario and Quebec since 1868.

By [proclaiming](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") the Royal Arms of Canada, King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") in 1921 made red and white the official colours of Canada; the former came from [Saint George's Cross](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross") and the latter from the French royal emblem since King [Charles VII](/wiki/Charles_VII_of_France "Charles VII of France").[[20]](#cite_note-birth-22) These colours became "entrenched" as the [national colours of Canada](/wiki/National_colours_of_Canada "National colours of Canada") upon the proclamation of the [Queen's personal Canadian flag](/wiki/Royal_flags_of_Canada#Sovereign "Royal flags of Canada") in 1962.[[21]](#cite_note-23) The [Department of Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Department_of_Canadian_Heritage "Department of Canadian Heritage") has listed the various colour shades for printing [ink](/wiki/Ink "Ink") that should be used when reproducing the Canadian flag; these include:[[9]](#cite_note-colours-11)

* FIP red: General Printing Ink, No. 0-712;
* [Inmont Canada Ltd](/w/index.php?title=Inmont_Canada_Ltd&action=edit&redlink=1 "Inmont Canada Ltd (page does not exist)")., No. 4T51577;
* [Monarch Inks](/w/index.php?title=Monarch_Inks&action=edit&redlink=1 "Monarch Inks (page does not exist)"), No. 62539/0
* [Rieger Inks](/w/index.php?title=Rieger_Inks&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rieger Inks (page does not exist)"), No. 25564
* Sinclair and Valentine, No. RL163929/0.

The number of points on the leaf has no special significance;[[22]](#cite_note-24) the number and arrangement of the points were chosen after wind tunnel tests showed the current design to be the least blurry of the various designs when tested under high-wind conditions.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The image of the maple leaf used on the flag was designed by Jacques Saint-Cyr;[[24]](#cite_note-Archbold,_Rick-26) Jack Cook claims that this stylized eleven-point maple leaf was lifted from a copyrighted design owned by a Canadian craft shop in Ottawa.[[25]](#cite_note-Eleven_Point-27) The colours 0/100/100/0 in the [CMYK](/wiki/CMYK "CMYK") process, PMS 032 (flag red 100%), or PMS 485 (used for screens) in the [Pantone](/wiki/Pantone "Pantone") colour specifier can be used when reproducing the flag.[[9]](#cite_note-colours-11) For the [Federal Identity Program](/wiki/Federal_Identity_Program "Federal Identity Program"), the red tone of the standard flag has an [RGB](/wiki/RGB "RGB") value of 255–0–0 ([web hexadecimal](/w/index.php?title=Web_hexadecimal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Web hexadecimal (page does not exist)") #FF0000).[[26]](#cite_note-28) In 1984, the National Flag of Canada Manufacturing Standards Act was passed to unify the manufacturing standards for flags used in both indoor and outdoor conditions.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

| Scheme | Red | White |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Pantone](/wiki/Pantone "Pantone") (Paper) | 032 C | White |
| [Web colours](/wiki/Web_colours "Web colours") | #EF3340 | #FFFFFF |
| [RGB](/wiki/RGB "RGB") | 239, 51, 64 | 255, 255, 255 |
| [CMYK](/wiki/CMYK "CMYK") | 0%, 79%, 73%, 6% | 0%, 0%, 0%, 0% |

The flag of Canada is represented as the [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") [emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji") sequence U+1F1E8 🇨 REGIONAL INDICATOR SYMBOL LETTER C, U+1F1E6 🇦 REGIONAL INDICATOR SYMBOL LETTER A: 🇨🇦.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early flags

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early flags")]

See also: [Historical flags of the British Empire and the overseas territories](/wiki/Historical_flags_of_the_British_Empire_and_the_overseas_territories "Historical flags of the British Empire and the overseas territories"), [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign"), and [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Jacques_Cartier_rencontre_les_indiens_de_Stadacone%2C_1535.jpg/250px-Jacques_Cartier_rencontre_les_indiens_de_Stadacone%2C_1535.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques_Cartier_rencontre_les_indiens_de_Stadacone,_1535.jpg)

Depiction of [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") meeting with [Iroquoians](/wiki/Iroquoian_peoples "Iroquoian peoples") at [Stadacona](/wiki/Stadacona "Stadacona"). Another member of Cartier's party is holding the [royal banner of France](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France").

The [Saint George's Cross](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") was carried by [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot") when he reached the later-named [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") in 1497. In 1534, [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") planted a cross in [Gaspé](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9_Peninsula "Gaspé Peninsula") bearing the French royal coat of arms with the [fleurs-de-lis](/wiki/Fleurs-de-lis "Fleurs-de-lis"). The [Royal Banner of France](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France") or "Bourbon Flag" held a position of some prominence in [New France](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"), with the evolving variations of [French military flags](/wiki/List_of_French_flags#Historical_flags "List of French flags") being used over time.[[29]](#cite_note-Association1915-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Flag_of_the_United_Empire_Loyalists.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Empire_Loyalists.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Empire_Loyalists.svg)

The [United Empire Loyalist](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") flag, which is similar to the [flag of Great Britain](/wiki/Flag_of_Great_Britain "Flag of Great Britain"), but in a 1:2 ratio. The flag is still used in [loyalist settlements](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist#List_of_Loyalist_settlements_in_Canada "United Empire Loyalist") within Canada.

As the *[de facto](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/de_facto "wikt:de facto")* British national flag, the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Jack#History "Union Jack") (commonly known as the "Union Jack") was used similarly in Canada from the time of British settlement in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") after 1621.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-Union_Flag-37) Its use continued after Canada's legislative independence from the United Kingdom in 1931 until the adoption of the current flag in 1965.[[6]](#cite_note-first_flags-8)[[36]](#cite_note-Canada.ca-38) The [United Empire Loyalist](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") flag, that is very similar to the Union Jack, was used by immigrants who remained [loyal to the British crown during the American Revolutionary War](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)"). In present-day Canada, the United Empire Loyalist flag continues to be used as symbol of pride and heritage for loyalist townships and organizations.[[37]](#cite_note-s891-39)

Shortly after [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867, the need for distinctive Canadian flags emerged. The first Canadian flag was then used as the [flag of the governor general of Canada](/wiki/Flag_of_the_governor_general_of_Canada "Flag of the governor general of Canada"), a Union Flag with a shield in the centre bearing the quartered arms of Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick, surrounded by a wreath of maple leaves.[[38]](#cite_note-Fraser_Flags-40) In 1870, the [Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign "Red Ensign"), with the addition of the Canadian composite shield in the fly, began to be used unofficially on land and sea[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) and was known as the *[Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign")*. As new provinces joined the Confederation, their arms were added to the shield. In 1892, the British admiralty approved the use of the Red Ensign for Canadian use at sea.[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Postcard_of_Maisonneuve_Monument_and_Coronation.JPG/250px-Postcard_of_Maisonneuve_Monument_and_Coronation.JPG)](/wiki/File:Postcard_of_Maisonneuve_Monument_and_Coronation.JPG)

A Canadian postcard marking the coronation of King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") in 1911, depicting a [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign") with a crowned composite shield of Canada in the fly, and the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") below it

The composite shield was replaced with the [coat of arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") upon its grant in 1921, and in 1924, an [Order in Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council") approved its use for Canadian government buildings abroad.[[6]](#cite_note-first_flags-8) In 1925, Prime Minister [William Lyon Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King") established a committee to design a flag to be used at home, but it was dissolved before the final report could be delivered. Despite the failure of the committee to solve the issue, public sentiment in the 1920s was in favour of fixing the flag problem for Canada.[[40]](#cite_note-42) New designs were proposed in 1927,[[41]](#cite_note-43) 1931,[[42]](#cite_note-44) and 1939.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

By the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the Red Ensign was viewed as Canada's *de facto* national flag. A joint committee of the Senate and House of Commons was appointed on November 8, 1945, to recommend a national flag to officially adopt. It received 2,409 designs from the public and was addressed by the director of the Historical Section of the Canadian Army, [Archer Fortescue Duguid](/wiki/Archer_Fortescue_Duguid "Archer Fortescue Duguid"), who pointed out that red and white were Canada's official colours and there was already an emblem representing the country: three joined maple leaves seen on the [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") of the Canadian coat of arms.[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) By May 9 the following year, the committee reported back with a recommendation "that the national flag of Canada should be the Canadian red ensign with a maple leaf in autumn golden colours in a bordered background of white". The [Legislative Assembly of Quebec](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Quebec "Legislative Assembly of Quebec") had urged the committee not to include any of what it deemed as "foreign symbols", including the Union Flag, and Mackenzie King, then still prime minister, declined to act on the report;[[20]](#cite_note-birth-22)[[38]](#cite_note-Fraser_Flags-40)[[44]](#cite_note-46) fearing it may lead to political instability. As a result, the Union Flag was kept as a national flag, and the order to fly the Canadian Red Ensign at government buildings was maintained.[[3]](#cite_note-canada-history-5)

### Great Flag Debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Flag Debate")]

Main article: [Great Canadian flag debate](/wiki/Great_Canadian_flag_debate "Great Canadian flag debate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Flag_of_Canada_%281957%E2%80%931965%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Canada_%281957%E2%80%931965%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Canada_(1957%E2%80%931965).svg)

The [Canadian Red Ensign](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign"), as used up to the Great Flag Debate

By the 1960s, the debate for an official Canadian flag intensified and became controversial, culminating in the Great Flag Debate of 1964.[[45]](#cite_note-47) In 1963, the minority [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") government of [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") gained power and decided to adopt a flag through parliamentary debate. The principal political proponent of the change was Pearson. He had been a significant broker during the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") of 1956, for which he was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize").[[46]](#cite_note-suez-48) During the crisis, Pearson was disturbed when the Egyptian government objected to [Canadian peacekeeping forces](/wiki/Canadian_peacekeeping "Canadian peacekeeping") because the Canadian flag (the Red Ensign) contained the same symbol (the Union Flag) also used by the United Kingdom, one of the belligerents.[[46]](#cite_note-suez-48) Pearson's goal was to create a flag that was distinctive and unmistakably Canadian. The main opponent to changing the flag was [John Diefenbaker](/wiki/John_Diefenbaker "John Diefenbaker"), [leader of the opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Opposition (Canada)") and former prime minister, who eventually made the subject a personal crusade.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Canada_Pearson_Pennant_1964.svg/250px-Canada_Pearson_Pennant_1964.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Canada_Pearson_Pennant_1964.svg)

A flag design created by [Alan Beddoe](/wiki/Alan_Beddoe "Alan Beddoe"), and dubbed the *Pearson Pennant*, being Prime Minister [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson")'s favoured design

In 1961, when Leader of the Opposition, Pearson asked [John Ross Matheson](/wiki/John_Ross_Matheson "John Ross Matheson"), a fellow Liberal MP, to begin researching what it would take for Canada to have a new flag. By April 1963, Pearson was prime minister in a minority government and risked losing power over the issue. He formed a 15-member multi-party parliamentary committee in 1963 to select a new design, despite opposition leader Diefenbaker's demands for a referendum on the issue.[[48]](#cite_note-50) On May 27, 1964, Pearson's cabinet introduced a motion to parliament for the adoption of his favourite design, presented to him by [Alan Beddoe](/wiki/Alan_Beddoe "Alan Beddoe"), an artist and heraldic advisor,[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) of a "sea to sea" (Canada's motto) flag with blue borders and three conjoined red maple leaves on a white field. This motion led to weeks of acrimonious debate in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"), and the design came to be known as the "Pearson Pennant",[[49]](#cite_note-51) derided by the media and viewed as a "concession to Quebec".[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Flag_of_Canada_%281964%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_Canada_%281964%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Canada_(1964).svg)

Flag design created by [George Stanley](/wiki/George_Stanley "George Stanley") and selected by the parliamentary committee

A new all-party committee was formed in September 1964, comprising seven Liberals, five Conservatives, one [New Democrat](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Canada "New Democratic Party of Canada"), one [Social Crediter](/wiki/Social_Credit_Party_of_Canada "Social Credit Party of Canada"), and one [Créditiste](/wiki/Ralliement_cr%C3%A9ditiste "Ralliement créditiste"), with [Herman Batten](/wiki/Herman_Maxwell_Batten "Herman Maxwell Batten") as chairman, while John Matheson acted as Pearson's right-hand man.[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) Among those who gave their opinions to the group was Duguid, expressing the same views as he had in 1945, insisting on a design using three maple leaves; [Arthur R. M. Lower](/wiki/Arthur_R._M._Lower "Arthur R. M. Lower"), stressing the need for a distinctly Canadian emblem; [Marcel Trudel](/wiki/Marcel_Trudel "Marcel Trudel"), arguing for symbols of Canada's founding nations, which did not include the maple leaf (a thought shared by Diefenbaker); and [A. Y. Jackson](/wiki/A._Y._Jackson "A. Y. Jackson"), providing his own suggested designs.[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) A steering committee also considered about 2,000 suggestions from the public, in addition to 3,900 others that included, according to [Library and Archives Canada](/wiki/Library_and_Archives_Canada "Library and Archives Canada"), "those that had accumulated in the Department of the Secretary of State and those from a parliamentary flag committee of 1945–1946".[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) Through six weeks of study with political manoeuvring, the committee took a vote on the two finalists: the Pearson Pennant (Beddoe's design) and the current design. Believing the Liberal members would vote for the prime minister's preference, the Conservatives voted for the single-leaf design. The Liberals, though, all voted for the single-leaf design, as did the members from the other two parties, giving a unanimous 15 to 0 vote[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) for the option created by [George Stanley](/wiki/George_Stanley "George Stanley") and inspired by the flag of the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada") (RMC) in [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario").[[50]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Flag_of_the_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada.svg)

Flag of the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada")

There, near the parade square, in March 1964, while viewing the college flag atop the Mackenzie Building, Stanley, RMC's Dean of Arts, first suggested to Matheson that the RMC flag should form the basis of the national flag. The suggestion was followed by Stanley's memorandum of March 23, 1964, on the history of Canada's emblems,[[51]](#cite_note-53) in which he warned that any new flag "must avoid the use of national or racial symbols that are of a divisive nature" and that it would be "clearly inadvisable" to create a flag that carried the Union Flag or a fleur-de-lis. According to Matheson, Pearson's "paramount and desperate objective" in introducing the new flag was keeping Quebec in Canada.[[52]](#cite_note-54) It was Stanley's idea that the new flag should be red and white and that it should feature the single maple leaf; his memorandum included the first sketch of what would become the flag of Canada. Stanley and Matheson collaborated on a design that was, after six months of debate and 308 speeches,[[39]](#cite_note-CE-41) passed by a majority vote in the House of Commons on December 15, 1964. Just after this, at 2 am, Matheson wrote to Stanley: "Your proposed flag has just now been approved by the Commons 163 to 78. Congratulations. I believe it is an excellent flag that will serve Canada well."[[53]](#cite_note-postcard-55) The [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") added its approval two days later.[[20]](#cite_note-birth-22)

### Proclamation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Proclamation")]

After the resolutions proposing a new national flag for Canada were passed by the two houses of parliament, a proclamation was drawn up for signature by the [Queen of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"). This was created in the form of an [illuminated](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") document on [vellum](/wiki/Vellum "Vellum"), with [calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy") by Yvonne Diceman and [heraldic](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry") illustrations. The text was rendered in black ink, using a [quill](/wiki/Quill "Quill"), while the heraldic elements were painted in [gouache](/wiki/Gouache "Gouache") with gilt highlights. The [Great Seal of Canada](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Canada "Great Seal of Canada") was embossed and secured by a silk ribbon.[[54]](#cite_note-Proc-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Proclamation_of_the_National_Flag_of_Canada_%28January_1965%29.jpg/250px-Proclamation_of_the_National_Flag_of_Canada_%28January_1965%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proclamation_of_the_National_Flag_of_Canada_(January_1965).jpg)

Royal proclamation of the national flag of Canada

This parchment was signed discreetly by the calligrapher but was made official by the signatures of Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") (given on January 28, 1965[[20]](#cite_note-birth-22)), [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister") [Lester Pearson](/wiki/Lester_Pearson "Lester Pearson"), and [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Canada "Attorney General of Canada") [Guy Favreau](/wiki/Guy_Favreau "Guy Favreau"). In order to obtain these signatures, the document was flown to the United Kingdom (for the Queen's [royal sign-manual](/wiki/Royal_sign-manual "Royal sign-manual")) and to the Caribbean (for the signature of Favreau, who was on vacation). This transport to different climates, combined with the quality of the materials with which the proclamation was created and the subsequent storage and repair methods (including the use of [Scotch Tape](/wiki/Scotch_Tape "Scotch Tape")), contributed to the deterioration of the document: The gouache was flaking off, leaving gaps in the heraldic designs, most conspicuously on the red maple leaf of the flag design in the centre of the sheet, and the adhesive from the tape had left stains. A desire to have the proclamation as part of a display at the [Canadian Museum of Civilization](/wiki/Canadian_Museum_of_History "Canadian Museum of History") marking the flag's 25th anniversary led to its restoration in 1989. The proclamation is today stored in a temperature and humidity-controlled plexiglass case to prevent the vellum from changing dimensionally.[[54]](#cite_note-Proc-56)

### Adoption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Adoption")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/The_Parliament_of_Canada_%2841122504821%29.jpg/250px-The_Parliament_of_Canada_%2841122504821%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Parliament_of_Canada_(41122504821).jpg)

The Canadian flag flying atop the [Peace Tower](/wiki/Peace_Tower "Peace Tower"). An official ceremony inaugurating the flag was held on Parliament Hill in 1965.

The new national flag was inaugurated on February 15, 1965, at an official ceremony held on [Parliament Hill](/wiki/Parliament_Hill "Parliament Hill") in Ottawa, in the presence of [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") Major-General [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier"), the Prime Minister, other members of the Cabinet, and Canadian parliamentarians. The Red Ensign was lowered at the stroke of noon, and the new maple leaf flag was raised. The crowd sang "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")" followed by "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen "God Save the Queen")".[[55]](#cite_note-flagfest-57) Of the flag, Vanier said "[it] will symbolize to each of us—and to the world—the unity of purpose and high resolve to which destiny beckons us".[[56]](#cite_note-Flap-58) [Maurice Bourget](/wiki/Maurice_Bourget "Maurice Bourget"), Speaker of the Senate, said: "The flag is the symbol of the nation's unity, for it, beyond any doubt, represents all the citizens of Canada without distinction of race, language, belief, or opinion."[[55]](#cite_note-flagfest-57) Yet there was still opposition to the change, and Stanley's life was even threatened for having "assassinated the flag". Despite this, Stanley attended the flag-raising ceremony.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

At the time of the 50th anniversary of the flag in 2015, the government—held by the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada")—was criticized for the lack of an official ceremony dedicated to the date; accusations of partisanship were levelled.[[56]](#cite_note-Flap-58) [Minister of Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Minister_of_Canadian_Heritage "Minister of Canadian Heritage") [Shelly Glover](/wiki/Shelly_Glover "Shelly Glover") denied the charges, and others, including Liberal Members of Parliament, pointed to community events taking place around the country.[[56]](#cite_note-Flap-58) Governor General [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)") did, though, preside at an official ceremony at [Confederation Park](/wiki/Confederation_Park "Confederation Park") in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), integrated with [Winterlude](/wiki/Winterlude "Winterlude"). He said, "[t]he National Flag of Canada is so embedded in our national life and so emblematic of our national purpose that we simply cannot imagine our country without it."[[58]](#cite_note-GG-60) Queen Elizabeth II stated: "On this, the 50th anniversary of the National Flag of Canada, I am pleased to join with all Canadians in the celebration of this unique and cherished symbol of our country and identity."[[59]](#cite_note-61) A commemorative stamp and coin were issued by [Canada Post](/wiki/Canada_Post "Canada Post") and the [Royal Canadian Mint](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mint "Royal Canadian Mint"), respectively.[[58]](#cite_note-GG-60)

## Alternative flags

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Alternative flags")]

As a symbol of the nation's membership in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and allegiance to [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), the [Royal Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") is an official Canadian flag and is flown on certain occasions.[[60]](#cite_note-royal_union-62) Regulations require federal installations to fly the Royal Union Flag beside the national flag when physically possible, using a second flagpole, on the following days: [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day") (the second Monday in March), [Victoria Day](/wiki/Victoria_Day "Victoria Day") (the same date as the [Canadian sovereign's official birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday#Canada "King's Official Birthday")), and the anniversary of the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") (December 11). The Royal Union Flag can also be flown at the [National War Memorial](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada) "National War Memorial (Canada)") or at other locations during ceremonies that honour Canadian involvement with forces of other Commonwealth nations during times of war. The national flag always precedes the Royal Union Flag, with the former occupying the place of honour.[[60]](#cite_note-royal_union-62)

The Royal Union Flag is also part of the provincial [flags of Ontario](/wiki/Flag_of_Ontario "Flag of Ontario") and [Manitoba](/wiki/Flag_of_Manitoba "Flag of Manitoba"), forming the canton of these flags; a stylized version is used on the [flag of British Columbia](/wiki/Flag_of_British_Columbia "Flag of British Columbia") and the [flag of Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Flag_of_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Flag of Newfoundland and Labrador").[[60]](#cite_note-royal_union-62) Several of the [provincial lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)") formerly used a modified union flag as their flags of office. The last to abolish this design was the [lieutenant governor of Nova Scotia](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_of_Nova_Scotia "Lieutenant governor of Nova Scotia") in 2024.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[60]](#cite_note-royal_union-62)

The [sovereign's flag for Canada](/wiki/Royal_flags_of_Canada#Sovereign "Royal flags of Canada") (banner of arms) is an official flag introduced May 6, 2023, and first flown at Government House, Halifax, that day.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

The Red Ensign is occasionally still used, including official use at some ceremonies. It was flown at the commemorations of the [Battle of Vimy Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimy_Ridge "Battle of Vimy Ridge") in 2007.[[63]](#cite_note-globe_red_ensign-65)[[64]](#cite_note-slams_red_ensign-66) This decision elicited criticism from those who believed it should not have been given equal status to the Canadian flag and received praise from people who believed that it was important to retain the ties to Canada's past.[[63]](#cite_note-globe_red_ensign-65)[[64]](#cite_note-slams_red_ensign-66) The Royal Union Flag and Red Ensign are still flown in Canada by veterans' groups and others who continue to stress the importance of Canada's British heritage and the Commonwealth connection.[[60]](#cite_note-royal_union-62)

In [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the [provincial flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Quebec "Flag of Quebec") (a white cross on a field of blue with four [fleurs-de-lis](/wiki/Fleurs-de-lis "Fleurs-de-lis")) can be considered a national flag along with the Maple Leaf flag, as is the [Acadian flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Acadia "Flag of Acadia") in the Acadian regions of the [Maritime provinces](/wiki/Maritime_provinces "Maritime provinces").[[24]](#cite_note-Archbold,_Rick-26)[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) Public display of Canadian flags is rare in Quebec, with most Quebecers preferring to fly the [flag of Quebec](/wiki/Flag_of_Quebec "Flag of Quebec") instead.[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71) Display of the flag is also contentious, with the Quebec provincial government ordering that the Quebec flag be given seniority over the Canadian one in the province,[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-73) and many Quebec government facilities, such as the [Quebec City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall_of_Quebec_City "City Hall of Quebec City"), the headquarters of the [Sûreté du Québec](/wiki/S%C3%BBret%C3%A9_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Sûreté du Québec") and [SAAQ](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_l%27assurance_automobile_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Société de l'assurance automobile du Québec"), and the [Quebec Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_Building_(Quebec) "Parliament Building (Quebec)"), refusing to fly the Canadian flag at all.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76)

## Protocol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Protocol")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/NB_-_Hartland_Bridge2.jpg/250px-NB_-_Hartland_Bridge2.jpg)](/wiki/File:NB_-_Hartland_Bridge2.jpg)

[Hartland Covered Bridge](/wiki/Hartland_Covered_Bridge "Hartland Covered Bridge"), with the Canadian flag on its tallest flagpole

No law dictates the proper use of the Canadian flag. Canadian Heritage has released rules for flying the National Flag of Canada alone and with other flags. The rules deal with the order of precedence in which the Canadian flag is placed, where the flag can be used, how it is used, and what people should do to honour the flag. The suggestions, titled *National Flag of Canada Etiquette,* were published by Canadian Heritage online and last updated on February 24, 2022.[[75]](#cite_note-77) [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") also have a unique protocol for folding the Canadian flag for presentations, such as during a funeral ceremony; CF does not recommend this method for everyday use.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

The flag can be displayed on any day at buildings operated by the Government of Canada, airports, military bases, diplomatic offices, and citizens during any time of the day. When flying the flag, it should be flown using its pole and should not be inferior to other flags, save for, in descending order, the [sovereign's flag for Canada](/wiki/Royal_flags_of_Canada#Sovereign "Royal flags of Canada"), the [governor general's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Flag of the Governor General of Canada"), any of the personal flags of members of the [Canadian Royal Family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Canada's_royal_family_and_house "Monarchy of Canada"), or flags of the [lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)").[[77]](#cite_note-79) The Canadian flag is [flown at half-mast in Canada](/wiki/Half-mast#Canada "Half-mast") to indicate a period of mourning.

## Promoting the flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Canada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Promoting the flag")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Items_from_the_Canadian_Parliamentary_Flag_Program.jpg/250px-Items_from_the_Canadian_Parliamentary_Flag_Program.jpg)](/wiki/File:Items_from_the_Canadian_Parliamentary_Flag_Program.jpg)

A sample of items from the Parliamentary Flag Program

Since the Canadian flag was adopted in 1965, the Canadian government has sponsored programs to promote it. Examples include the Canadian Parliamentary Flag Program of the [Department of Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Department_of_Canadian_Heritage "Department of Canadian Heritage") and the flag program run by the [Department of Public Works](/wiki/Public_Works_and_Government_Services_Canada "Public Works and Government Services Canada"). These programs increased the exposure of the flag and the concept that it was part of the national identity. To raise awareness of the new flag, the Parliamentary Flag Program was set up in December 1972 by the Cabinet and, beginning in 1973,[[78]](#cite_note-80) allowed members of the House of Commons to distribute flags and lapel pins in the shape of the Canadian flag to their constituents. Full-size flags that have been flown on [Peace Tower](/wiki/Peace_Tower "Peace Tower") and four other locations on [Parliament Hill](/wiki/Parliament_Hill "Parliament Hill") are packaged by the Department of Public Works and offered to the public free of charge. As of March 2019, the program has a waiting list of over 100 years for both Peace Tower flags, which are 2.3 by 4.6 metres (7.5 by 15 ft) in size, and for flags from the other four locations (one on each side of [Centre Block](/wiki/Centre_Block "Centre Block") and one each over [East](/wiki/East_Block "East Block") and [West](/wiki/West_Block "West Block") Blocks), which are 1.4 by 2.7 metres (4.5 by 9 ft).[[79]](#cite_note-Parliament-Flag-Request-81)

Since 1996, February 15 has been commemorated as [National Flag of Canada Day](/wiki/National_Flag_of_Canada_Day "National Flag of Canada Day").[[55]](#cite_note-flagfest-57) In 1996, Minister of Canadian Heritage [Sheila Copps](/wiki/Sheila_Copps "Sheila Copps") instituted the One in a Million National Flag Challenge.[[80]](#cite_note-82) Canadian Heritage put the expenses at $15.5 million, with approximately a seventh of the cost offset by donations.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

In February 2025, in the lead up to the 60th anniversary of the Canadian maple leaf flag, former prime ministers [Kim Campbell](/wiki/Kim_Campbell "Kim Campbell"), [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien"), [Joe Clark](/wiki/Joe_Clark "Joe Clark"), [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") and [Paul Martin](/wiki/Paul_Martin "Paul Martin") encouraged Canadians to show national pride and fly the flag "like never before" in the light of "threats and insults" against Canadian Sovereignty by United States President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[[82]](#cite_note-84)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
